paragraphs.text: Avoiding duplication: Many topics addressed in this consultation are already addressed through existing regulation. New legal obligations should not create overlap with existing laws.

section_path: Executive Summary: Samsung Electronics' DFA Position

paragraphs.text: Targeted Action: European Commission (EC) should avoid making non-problematic actors collateral damage of blanket bans or restrictions.

section_path: Executive Summary: Samsung Electronics' DFA Position

paragraphs.text: Structured Risk-Based Approach: When the Digital Fairness Act (DFA) introduces new obligations, these should follow a clearly-defined process for companies to (1) identify potentially harmful practices, (2) determine the risk level, and (3) take mitigating actions.

section_path: Executive Summary: Samsung Electronics' DFA Position

paragraphs.text: Clear definitions with risk-based approach: Clearly defining what practices are problematic, but also letting businesses assess whether their practices truly result in consumer harm and take appropriate action.

section_path: Executive Summary: Samsung Electronics' DFA Position

paragraphs.text: Protection of Minors: SE takes protection of minors from addictive designs seriously; SE welcomes work done already through Article 28 Digital Services Act (DSA) Guidance and hopes a similar risk-based approach is used for future EC action in this area.

evidence_quotes.quote: "addictive designs"

paragraphs.text: Differentiation needed: Not all practices EC places under category of "addictive designs" are actually harmful – companies should be able to self-differentiate.

Due to innovations and variety in the usage of influencer marketing, principles-based regulation is appropriate.

evidence_quotes.quote: Many problematic pricing practices are already addressed through existing regulation.

paragraphs.text: Avoiding duplication: Many problematic pricing practices are already addressed through existing regulation. New legal obligations should not create overlap with existing laws and be risk-based.

section_path: Executive Summary: Samsung Electronics' DFA Position

evidence_quotes.quote: which information should be shared with consumers with which level of visibility

paragraphs.text: Clarity on information, not on specific technical processes: The DFA should explain which information should be shared with consumers with which level of visibility, but not mandate technical processes for how that information is shared with the consumer.

section_path: Executive Summary: Samsung Electronics' DFA Position

evidence_quotes.quote: "fairness by design"

paragraphs.text: Reducing reporting burdens: Suggestions to introduce "fairness by design" and to reduce the burden of proof would run contrary to the EC's push for reporting burden reduction and harm businesses, especially smaller ones.

section_path: Executive Summary: Samsung Electronics' DFA Position

paragraphs.text: As a global technology brand and market leader in various technological sectors, Samsung Electronics (SE) takes consumer protection very seriously. Consumer trust is fundamental to SE's business. Therefore, not only adherence to, but often going beyond what is legally mandated in existing consumer protection law is a top priority for our company. This is particularly the case when it comes to protection of minors and other groups of vulnerable consumers. Therefore, SE hopes that the EC will work with companies to ensure new obligations introduced via the DFA still allow us to deliver the products and services our customers have come to expect.

section_path: Samsung Electronics as a Trusted Consumer Brand

paragraphs.text: As will be highlighted throughout this contribution, rules to protect consumers in the digital ecosystem already exist in legislation like the Unfair Consumer Practices Directive (UCPD), the Price Indications Directive (PID), the Consumer Rights Directive (CRD), the Digital Services Act (DSA), the Digital Content Directive (DCD) and the General Data Protection Regulation (GDPR). SE would urge the EC to avoid overlap with obligations in these existing laws when drafting the DFA.

Reading the survey prompts for this consultation, each section highlights specific digital services, -platforms, -products, or -practices the EC is concerned about. In many cases, it also seems the EC has specific companies in mind whose practices they see as problematic.

evidence_quotes.quote: correcting actions of those particular problematic actors

paragraphs.text: In the event that the EC should take legislative action, SE would urge precision and directness instead of broad, blunt instruments. By presenting new legislation that introduces blanket bans or restrictions, the EC risks making non-problematic companies collateral damage, letting a small number of "bad actors" decide the digital economy. SE would hope that following this consultation, the EC focuses on correcting actions of those particular problematic actors, rather than harming the broader digital ecosystem.

evidence_quotes.quote: protection of minors

paragraphs.text: Therefore, SE would request the EC follows a risk-based approach to actions taken after this consultation along the model it recently used for its Guidelines on application of the DSA's Article 28 on protection of minors.

To be more specific on this risk-based approach, SE urges the EC to use a clear, structured methodology to allow companies to effectively mitigate any risks identified under the DFA. Concretely, this risk-based approach should be structured as:
A clear list or specific definition of problematic practices the EC is trying to address via the DFA. Such a list could be drafted via the EC's ongoing DFA impact assessments.
A template for companies to conduct a risk assessment for the potential harm a given practice could cause.
Proposed mitigation measures according to the level of risk identified.
As noted above, only the most obviously negative behaviors, which do not have redeeming features, and which always result in consumer harm, should find their way onto a list of banned practices.

By adopting this approach across the entirety of the DFA, the EC would simplify compliance for companies. This clearly-documented process enables companies to demonstrate that they've followed the correct steps to address potential risk when dealing with regulatory authorities.

evidence_quotes.quote: Dark patterns are a prime example of practices that are already covered by existing legislation

paragraphs.text: Dark patterns are a prime example of practices that are already covered by existing legislation, but there is a lack of coordination and enforcement across these laws. New obligations under the DFA should take these parts of legislation where dark patterns are already covered into account:

evidence_quotes.quote: Online platforms shall not deceive or manipulate consumers through interface design.
Gives a broad definition of dark patterns and gives non-exhaustive list of practices that can be considered dark patterns.

paragraphs.text: | Law | Location | Text (Summary) | | --- | --- | --- | | DSA | Recital 67 | Gives a broad definition of dark patterns and gives non-exhaustive list of practices that can be considered dark patterns. | | | Article 25(1) | Online platforms shall not deceive or manipulate consumers through interface design. | | GDPR | Article 7 | Defining conditions for data subjects to consent to data processing. | | UCPD | Articles 6, 7 | Outlaws misleading commercial practices. | | | Annex I | List of commercial practices considered “unfair” (and therefore are considered dark patterns). |

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Many of the recent developments around dark patterns or behavior harmful to consumers reflect new instances of previously identified harms, rather than brand new ones. The most appropriate response is to address them in updating the UCPD guidance, rather than adding to compliance burdens by instituting new rules.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: In any case, further regulating or enforcing against dark patterns would require clear definitions to differentiate problematic practices from legitimate interface design techniques. SE does not oppose enforcement against legitimately harmful dark patterns. However, given that definitions are currently strewn across different laws

evidence_quotes.quote: dark pattern

paragraphs.text: with significant overlap, companies need a clear definition of what a dark pattern is for effective enforcement. Once this happens, companies will be able to design or redesign their interfaces confidently knowing what falls in and out of scope.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: Giving a clear definition of dark patterns is one part of effective enforcement, but there will always be cases that fall in a gray area. Therefore, companies should also have the opportunity, via risk assessments, to determine whether their interface designs are indeed harmful, or whether there are legitimate reasons to design their interface a certain way. This way, companies can continue to innovate knowing where the boundaries of interface design are. In any case, only the most clearly and unequivocally harmful practices should be subject to a ban (i.e. adding them to Annex I of the UCPD).

evidence_quotes.quote: interface design decisions below:

paragraphs.text: To highlight some cases in this potential "gray area," please find some examples of interface design decisions below:
A smartphone user is periodically reminded to install a software update, e.g. for security reasons.

evidence_quotes.quote: consent management platform

paragraphs.text: Smartphones will also periodically remind users to reevaluate their data privacy preferences via a consent management platform.
Consumers are offered the opportunity to trade in a device or purchase device insurance both on the product page and checkout phase of the online purchase journey.

evidence_quotes.quote: dark patterns

paragraphs.text: All three cases fall into a gray area for dark patterns and show why context (and a risk-based approach) in interface design is important.

evidence_quotes.quote: Users are periodically reminded to update their device or software applications.
While this may fall under the technical definition of "nudging" or "click fatigue," the repeated suggestion is in the user's interest, as updates bring important security updates or patches.

paragraphs.text: Users are periodically reminded to update their device or software applications. If they do not wish to do so, they have to reject the update via a click. While this may fall under the technical definition of "nudging" or "click fatigue," the repeated suggestion is in the user's interest, as updates bring important security updates or patches.

evidence_quotes.quote: prompting users on a regular basis is not with the goal of "nudging" consumers in a certain direction.

paragraphs.text: In the case of data privacy preferences, prompting users on a regular basis is not with the goal of "nudging" consumers in a certain direction. Rather, this practice is in accordance with GDPR best practices, ensuring that consent to process personal data remains valid.

evidence_quotes.quote: the mere fact that a potential add-on is presented twice does not constitute nudging or click fatigue (or confirm-shaming).

paragraphs.text: For the offer of trade-in or device insurance, the mere fact that a potential add-on is presented twice does not constitute nudging or click fatigue (or confirm-shaming). This practice is not harmful as long as the language used is neutral and the consumer is able to intuitively make their desired choice without dead-ends or unnecessary extra steps.

evidence_quotes.quote: Much like with dark patterns, SE believes a risk-based approach to addictive design features is the correct way to minimize harm to consumers.

paragraphs.text: Much like with dark patterns, SE believes a risk-based approach to addictive design features is the correct way to minimize harm to consumers. That is why SE welcomes the DSA guidelines on Article 28 on the protection of minors. Those actors who pose the highest threat to minors' wellbeing online should have the most rigorous child protection features. The same should be the case when it comes to addictive designs.

evidence_quotes.quote: reducing effects of addictive designs.

paragraphs.text: Protection of minors is a joint effort by all actors involved in the digital ecosystem, so SE implements a series of safety features for minors and parents. Some of these features are specifically aimed at reducing effects of addictive designs.
Screen time limits for children, programmable by guardians in smartphone settings.

section_path: Select Overview of Samsung Electronics' Child Protection Features

paragraphs.text: Tools for monitoring children's phone usage.

section_path: Select Overview of Samsung Electronics' Child Protection Features

paragraphs.text: Restrictions on access to apps within the phone.

section_path: Select Overview of Samsung Electronics' Child Protection Features

evidence_quotes.quote: limiting access/download to age-appropriate apps in the Galaxy application store.

paragraphs.text: Management of app store purchase authorizations and limiting access/download to age-appropriate apps in the Galaxy application store.

section_path: Select Overview of Samsung Electronics' Child Protection Features

evidence_quotes.quote: "addictive design"

paragraphs.text: Beyond protection of minors, SE also does not consider all practices that the EC would place under the category of "addictive design" harmful. Therefore, SE would urge the EC to clearly delineate between harmful and non-harmful practices. An example below on how a design element generally classified by the EC as "addictive" is not problematic.

section_path: Select Overview of Samsung Electronics' Child Protection Features

paragraphs.text: Autoplay of promotional videos in a software application store

It can hardly be argued that a main concern to be tackled by the DFA is time spent on software application stores. According to data gathered by the B2B media and information platform “Business of Apps,” users spend an average of 10 seconds on any single app store page, with 80% of Android users scrolling to only 10% of an app store page’s entire length.

evidence_quotes.quote: automatically-played promotional videos

paragraphs.text: It is therefore difficult to argue that automatically-played promotional videos are “addictive” even if they technically fit the definition of autoplay. Furthermore, these agreements to autoplay promotional videos are an important source of revenue for app store owners.
Autoplay as an educational feature

evidence_quotes.quote: a video may play automatically that demonstrates how to use the app.

paragraphs.text: When users enter an application, particularly one they are using for the first time, a video may play automatically that demonstrates how to use the app. To users that are new to an application and unfamiliar with its features or interface, this may be a helpful tool that enables them to save time and have a better user experience.

evidence_quotes.quote: SE does believe that recommender systems should be easily switched off if the user chooses to do so.

However, if the DFA were to oblige all recommender systems to be switched “off” by default, this would once again make non-problematic (or in this case, non-addictive) recommender systems collateral damage.

paragraphs.text: Also here, based on the suggestions put forward in the DFA survey, it is clear the EC is concerned about specific types of recommender systems – most likely those on social media. SE does believe that recommender systems should be easily switched off if the user chooses to do so. However, if the DFA were to oblige all recommender systems to be switched “off” by default, this would once again make non-problematic (or in this case, non-addictive) recommender systems collateral damage.

evidence_quotes.quote: recommender systems

paragraphs.text: Rather, the EC should clearly define what types of recommender systems it considers problematic and allow providers of recommender systems to risk-assess their service along a clearly-defined process. This way, high-risk recommender systems will have to take appropriate measures to minimize risk to consumers, while low-risk recommender systems can continue offering consumers a better experience on the products and services they use without being subject to additional checks or obligations under the DFA.

evidence_quotes.quote: Further regulating recommender systems

paragraphs.text: Further regulating recommender systems also presents a potential risk of duplication, as the Platform Regulation (2019/1150) and DSA already include targeted rules on transparency.

evidence_quotes.quote: addictive for users.

paragraphs.text: Some examples of recommender systems that have a relatively low level of danger for being addictive for users.

evidence_quotes.quote: based on dietary preferences

paragraphs.text: Smart refrigerators can recommend recipes to users based on dietary preferences, nutritional goals, cooking skill level, and household size indicated during device setup.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalized (what apps users previously downloaded)

paragraphs.text: Recommendations for download are both personalized (what apps users previously downloaded) and context-based (back-to-school, holidays, sports events, etc.) to show users the most relevant applications to them.

evidence_quotes.quote: This system actually brings users to apps they may be looking for in the quickest way possible, as opposed to keeping them on the app store as long as possible.

paragraphs.text: This system actually brings users to apps they may be looking for in the quickest way possible, as opposed to keeping them on the app store as long as possible.

evidence_quotes.quote: risks to consumers in video games

paragraphs.text: As has been the case throughout this position, SE believes that outright bans or restrictions are not the right approach. That being said, SE agrees that ensuring vulnerable groups like minors are adequately protected should be a top priority of the DFA. Beyond that, SE hopes the EC would minimize the risks to consumers in video games while also balancing this with the enjoyability/replay value of games.

section_path: IV. Specific Features in Video Games

evidence_quotes.quote: Regarding minor protection, many video games or platforms that distribute games, are already subject to the child protection requirements under Article 28 DSA.
Moreover, parents can restrict their children from making purchases of games or in the apps themselves.
setting clearer rules via the DFA for how children should specifically be protected in video game environments would be welcomed.

paragraphs.text: Regarding minor protection, many video games or platforms that distribute games, are already subject to the child protection requirements under Article 28 DSA. Additionally, as mentioned above, SE's own parental control features allow parents to limit access to games for their children in a set age range. Moreover, parents can restrict their children from making purchases of games or in the apps themselves. Otherwise, setting clearer rules via the DFA for how children should specifically be protected in video game environments would be welcomed.

section_path: IV. Specific Features in Video Games

evidence_quotes.quote: uncertainty-based rewards and in-game currencies are optional, not mandatory.
Purchase of in-game currency and uncertainty-based rewards support the development of games for studios, particularly smaller ones.

paragraphs.text: Regarding adult users, maintaining free choice is important as items like uncertainty-based rewards and in-game currencies are optional, not mandatory. Purchase of in-game currency and uncertainty-based rewards support the development of games for studios, particularly smaller ones. Developers should be able to design fun, innovative, engaging in-game products (like uncertainty-based rewards) as long as adult consumers are adequately informed when they make purchases.

section_path: IV. Specific Features in Video Games

paragraphs.text: Then, some of the concerns raised by the EC in the consultation are already addressed via existing regulation. SE would urge caution in duplicating regulatory obligations:

section_path: IV. Specific Features in Video Games

evidence_quotes.quote: Games may not “omit material information that the average consumer needs” in the event of a purchase (applied in this case: digital currency purchase, uncertainty-based reward purchase).

paragraphs.text: | Law | Location | Text (Summary) | | --- | --- | --- | | UCPD | Article 7 | Games may not “omit material information that the average consumer needs” in the event of a purchase (applied in this case: digital currency purchase, uncertainty-based reward purchase). | | DCD | Recital 23 | Digital representations of value (in-app currency) should be considered a method of payment within the context of the DCD – and therefore already regulated. |

section_path: IV. Specific Features in Video Games

evidence_quotes.quote: transparency for in-game purchases
probabilities of outcomes in uncertainty-based rewards
value of purchase content

paragraphs.text: Finally, many of the points raised in the consultation are already addressed via self-regulation, specifically the PEGI (Pan-European Game Information) Code of Conduct. The PEGI rating system is an industry standard for age-appropriateness, and only developers that have signed the Code of Conduct may use the system. It addresses topics directly relevant to the DFA like transparency for in-game purchases, probabilities of outcomes in uncertainty-based rewards, and value of purchase content.

evidence_quotes.quote: age assurance measures for minors to ensure they are not circumventing parental controls

paragraphs.text: This Code of Conduct has heavy buy-in from the industry. As the EC knows through its own codes of conduct, they are effective methods to ensure “good behavior” when there is industry buy-in. Should the EC seek a way to enforce the measures in the Code of Conduct via the DFA, SE would encourage a similar approach to the Guidelines for application of Article 28 DSA – both in terms of the risk-based approach and the age assurance measures for minors to ensure they are not circumventing parental controls.

evidence_quotes.quote: there is already a comprehensive legal framework governing collection- and processing of personal data, as well as personalization practices.

paragraphs.text: As is the case with other parts of this consultation, SE would like to point out that there is already a comprehensive legal framework governing collection- and processing of personal data, as well as personalization practices. Therefore, SE would hope that the EC carefully examines what is already covered to ensure there is no overlap in regulation. Some examples of the existing regulatory framework:

| Law | Location | Text (Summary) | | --- | --- | --- | | GDPR | Articles 5, 6 | General principles of processing data, what qualifies as lawful data processing. | | | Articles 12, 13 | Clear communication of data use to data subjects. |

evidence_quotes.quote: Prohibiting use of sensitive personal data for advertising purposes.
Transparency of recommender systems.
Prohibiting personalized advertising to minors.
Guidance on age assurance mechanisms, age-appropriate design, recommender systems, protecting minors from persuasive commercial practices.

paragraphs.text: | | Article 21 | Right for data subjects to object to data processing/direct marketing. | | --- | --- | --- | | DSA | Article 26(3) | Prohibiting use of sensitive personal data for advertising purposes. | | | Article 27 | Transparency of recommender systems. | | | Article 28 | Prohibiting personalized advertising to minors. | | | Article 28 Guidance | Guidance on age assurance mechanisms, age-appropriate design, recommender systems, protecting minors from persuasive commercial practices. |

evidence_quotes.quote: In many cases, personalization is used to improve consumer experiences on devices or services that users have already purchased.
By forcing companies to switch off personalized recommendations by default, or restricting personalized data processing beyond the current legal framework, consumers would miss out on these benefits.

paragraphs.text: In many cases, personalization is used to improve consumer experiences on devices or services that users have already purchased. In fact, certain personalization practices can actually lead to consumers spending less money than they would have otherwise. By forcing companies to switch off personalized recommendations by default, or restricting personalized data processing beyond the current legal framework, consumers would miss out on these benefits. Some examples:

evidence_quotes.quote: consent before receiving personalized recommendations.

paragraphs.text: > All SE product/service recommendations for home appliances are opt-in based, so a user has to consent before receiving personalized recommendations.
Within SE's Home care service, consumers can save money on homeowners' insurance.

evidence_quotes.quote: discounted rates based on the smart home appliances the consumer owns.

paragraphs.text: Consumers who own SE smart appliances are paired with insurers that offer discounted rates based on the smart home appliances the consumer owns.
Personalized recommendations for ideal charging times for smart home-connected devices based on peak/off-peak hours.
Automated recommendation to switch off smart home devices when no one is home.
Personalized usage recommendations per smart home device based on a pre-defined budget for energy.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalization

paragraphs.text: Then, looking at personalization from a more "traditional" advertising angle, SE believes that when applied within the applicable legal framework, personalized

section_path: Personalization as a Driver of Economic Competitiveness

evidence_quotes.quote: personalized advertising
prompt users "out-of-box" (when first using a product or service) on whether they want personalized advertising.

paragraphs.text: advertising is an important driver of growth for the European economy. Therefore, the DFA should not seek to restrict personalized advertising or mandate that it be switched off by default. Rather, the EC should continue to allow companies to prompt users "out-of-box" (when first using a product or service) on whether they want personalized advertising.

section_path: Personalization as a Driver of Economic Competitiveness

evidence_quotes.quote: personalization on SE services is opt-in only.
minors are not served targeted advertising.

paragraphs.text: > **Samsung Electronics' Approach to Personalization** > > Consumer choice is paramount, which is why personalization on SE services is opt-in only. SE customers can also always easily switch off personalization. Additionally, SE re-prompts users in regular intervals to ensure their preferences have remained the same and give them the option to adjust their settings if they wish to do so. > > In the case of minors, SE has robust policies to ensure data is not collected on minors on any of our services and that minors are not served targeted advertising.

section_path: Personalization as a Driver of Economic Competitiveness

evidence_quotes.quote: When consumers do choose to receive personalized content and advertising, this is a major driver of growth for the European economy.

paragraphs.text: When consumers do choose to receive personalized content and advertising, this is a major driver of growth for the European economy. Personalization ensures that businesses can reach the right customers while saving commercial resources – something particularly important to small- and medium-sized enterprises (SMEs). Data from a 2025 study conducted by Implement Consulting Group, commissioned by Google revealed some important statistics about personalized advertising:

section_path: Personalization as a Driver of Economic Competitiveness

paragraphs.text: Personalized ads were responsible for 100 billion euros in additional sales for EU businesses in 2023.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalized ads

paragraphs.text: Particularly smaller companies benefit from personalized ads, securing 80 billion euros in extra revenue in 2023 – with 75% of SMEs saying they would struggle to find customers without personalized advertising.
76% of SMEs surveyed agreed that personalized ads allow them to compete with larger businesses.
Just over half of consumers (52%) said they prefer ads relevant to them over context-based ads.
Just over half of consumers (56%) agreed that they prefer ads relevant to them over random ads.

evidence_quotes.quote: Personalized ads help keep certain services free to use for consumers

paragraphs.text: Personalized ads help keep certain services free to use for consumers – for example: 80% of Europeans believe it's important to access news content online, but only 30% are willing to pay for this content).

Also in this category, many of the topics of concern raised by the EC in the DFA consultation are already addressed through existing law. Therefore, SE would hope that the EC carefully examines what is already covered to ensure there is no overlap in regulation. Some examples of the existing regulatory framework:

evidence_quotes.quote: Detailed information on which practices are or are not permitted for influencers.
Also, detailing how disclosures should be structured.

paragraphs.text: | Law | Location | Text (Summary) | | --- | --- | --- | | UCPD | Annex I, Number 11 | Advertisements need to be clearly labeled. | | 2021 EC Guidance on UPCD | Section 4.2.6 | Detailed information on which practices are or are not permitted for influencers. Also, detailing how disclosures should be structured. | | DSA | Article 26 | Requirement for online platforms displaying ads to clearly label them as such and provide meaningful information about the advertiser. |

For additional safeguards the EC wants to introduce beyond the current legal framework, SE calls on the EC to work with industry to ensure any legal obligations closely align with existing industry guidelines. In SE's case, company internal guidelines already meet or exceed advertising standards worldwide. Companies like

evidence_quotes.quote: influencers working with us adhere to applicable laws.

paragraphs.text: SE have a responsibility to our customers to ensure influencers working with us adhere to applicable laws. Without industry involvement, the introduction of new obligations which significantly differ from current best practices would raise administrative costs for businesses, particularly smaller ones.

evidence_quotes.quote: "brands and agencies [take] measures to ensure influencers comply with legal obligations,"

paragraphs.text: To give a bit more clarity on what company-led guidelines already do, and to respond to the part of the consultation suggesting that "brands and agencies [take] measures to ensure influencers comply with legal obligations," here are some examples of SE's own policies:
Select Overview of Samsung Electronics' Influencer Marketing Policies

evidence_quotes.quote: they must disclose their paid partnership content.

paragraphs.text: Disclosure: For influencers to work with SE, they must disclose their paid partnership content. They must use the platform disclosure tools available to them (such as: "Paid Partnership with Samsung Mobile")

evidence_quotes.quote: Compliance with applicable law: Influencers have an obligation to comply with local regulations such as the ARPP rules in France and the Wettbewerbszentrale Requirements in Germany.

paragraphs.text: Compliance with applicable law: Influencers have an obligation to comply with local regulations such as the ARPP rules in France and the Wettbewerbszentrale Requirements in Germany.

evidence_quotes.quote: Every paid influencer asset is reviewed by SE or its agencies before going live to ensure correct disclosure

paragraphs.text: Content pre-approval process: Every paid influencer asset is reviewed by SE or its agencies before going live to ensure correct disclosure, accurate representation of the product, and adherence to brand safety requirements.

evidence_quotes.quote: All influencer agreements include clear contractual clauses covering compliance with SE's policies and legal obligations.

paragraphs.text: Contractual safeguards: All influencer agreements include clear contractual clauses covering compliance with SE's policies and legal obligations.

evidence_quotes.quote: Active monitoring: SE tracks influencer activity through a mix of manual checks and digital monitoring tools to ensure adherence to guidelines.

paragraphs.text: Active monitoring: SE tracks influencer activity through a mix of manual checks and digital monitoring tools to ensure adherence to guidelines.
VII. Pricing Practices

evidence_quotes.quote: pricing for goods or services

paragraphs.text: To avoid overlap or duplication in regulation of pricing for goods or services, here is an overview of some of the practices that are already regulated in relation to the practices highlighted in the DFA consultation survey. SE would hope the EC takes these existing rules into account.
| Law | Location | Text (Summary) | | --- | --- | --- | | PID | Article 6a (via Article 2(1) Directive 2019/2161) | Price reductions should indicate prior price applied over past 30 days. |
| CRD | Article 6(1)(e) | Traders shall display the total price of goods or services in a clear and comprehensible manner. | | --- | --- | --- |

evidence_quotes.quote: advertising price discounts.
the lowest price charged in the preceding 30 days.

paragraphs.text: To be more specific on what already is regulated: the PID (98/6) was amended as part of the Omnibus package (Directive 2019/2161) to introduce clearer rules on advertising price discounts. With this amendment, the EC clarified that any announcement of a price reduction had to show the lowest price charged in the preceding 30 days. SE deems it unnecessary to introduce further obligations in this area under the DFA.

evidence_quotes.quote: On so-called "drip-pricing," where all mandatory fees and delivery charges are not made clear to the user,
final pricing information needs to be provided to consumers (such as at the product page or "invitation to purchase" stage)

paragraphs.text: On so-called "drip-pricing," where all mandatory fees and delivery charges are not made clear to the user, the CRD (2011/83) already applies in this area. However, if the EC intends to specify exactly when the final pricing information needs to be provided to consumers (such as at the product page or "invitation to purchase" stage), a slight amendment of the UCPD may be appropriate.

evidence_quotes.quote: personalized pricing

paragraphs.text: Finally, if the Commission takes action in respect of personalized pricing, any changes should be risk-based, allowing companies to self-assess risk.

evidence_quotes.quote: consumers should be able to cancel digital subscriptions through a simple process.

paragraphs.text: SE agrees that information to consumers about digital contracts should be clear and that consumers should be able to cancel digital subscriptions through a simple process.

evidence_quotes.quote: about their subscriptions
consumer-friendly "unsubscribe" or "cancellation" pathway

paragraphs.text: SE would hope that the EC maintains technological neutrality when addressing the cancellation of contracts in the DFA. In this case, "technological neutrality" would mean the DFA confirms which information companies need to share with consumers about their subscriptions and what level of visibility that information needs to be given. Then, companies must implement a consumer-friendly "unsubscribe" or "cancellation" pathway that complies with the boundaries set out in the DFA. SE would welcome this solution as it would take into account the diverse internal systems companies have for processing and managing customer subscriptions.

evidence_quotes.quote: SE manages subscriptions via a centralized payment system

paragraphs.text: SE manages subscriptions via a centralized payment system, which the different subscription-based services from different parts of the business connect to.

evidence_quotes.quote: their click takes them to this centralized payment system interface, where the actual action of unsubscribing takes place with a second click.

paragraphs.text: When a customer chooses to unsubscribe, their click takes them to this centralized payment system interface, where the actual action of unsubscribing takes place with a second click.

evidence_quotes.quote: unsubscribing unnecessarily difficult

paragraphs.text: SE is not making unsubscribing unnecessarily difficult, but rather has an entirely legitimate reason to design the consumer journey in this way.

evidence_quotes.quote: the right to communicate with a person in case of a problem with their contract is a valid obligation to include in the DFA.
SE would urge the EC to not mandate the means to communicate with a person.

paragraphs.text: Then, SE believes the right to communicate with a person in case of a problem with their contract is a valid obligation to include in the DFA. SE customers have the option to communicate with customer support via email, by phone, and in store. However, much like the point made above about the customer journey, SE would urge the EC to not mandate the means to communicate with a person.

This current EC administration has stated its goal to simplify legislation, particularly when it comes to reporting requirements. With numerous omnibus packages harmonizing, consolidating, or reducing reporting requirements, SE does not understand why the EC would take a different approach to consumer protection.

evidence_quotes.quote: document their decisions for practices included in the DFA.

items.topic: Reversal Of The Burden Of Proof

evidence_quotes.quote: "reversal of the burden of proof"

paragraphs.text: By adopting the risk-based approach SE has been advocating throughout this position, businesses would already have to document their decisions for practices included in the DFA. However, going beyond the documentation resulting from a risk-based approach to satisfy the "reversal of the burden of proof" would increase compliance costs and bureaucracy for businesses across the board.

evidence_quotes.quote: On "fairness by design," companies would have to receive some form of certification to prove their compliance.

paragraphs.text: On "fairness by design," companies would have to receive some form of certification to prove their compliance. Defining the right standards for such a certification would be very extremely time-consuming and resource-intensive. This would further increase costs and administrative burden for businesses. SE believes the EC should not take these kinds of sweeping measures, but rather address specific risks identified in the DFA consultation in a targeted manner.
The contribution of the Central European Lawyers Initiative (CELI) is provided in the attached document.
